summary: Spolsky follows up on his criticism of Windows Vista's shutdown menu by citing insider Moishe Lettvin's revelation that 24 people were involved in implementing that single feature. He uses this as evidence that Microsoft has become hopelessly entangled in bureaucracy, overstaffing, and management layers. He argues that Microsoft's rapid hiring forced them to lower their standards significantly. The irony, he notes, is that Microsoft once looked at IBM's bloated OS/2 team as a cautionary tale, only to become the same kind of bloated monster that takes five years to ship an incoherent product.
key_points: A single shutdown menu feature required 24 people to implement, illustrating massive organizational dysfunction
Microsoft's growth forced it to lower hiring standards significantly to fill positions
The company became consumed by bureaucracy, management layers, and endless meetings
Microsoft ironically became the same kind of bloated organization (like IBM's OS/2 team) it once used as a cautionary example
money_quotes: Every piece of evidence I've heard from developers inside Microsoft supports my theory that the company has become completely tangled up in bureaucracy, layers of management, meetings ad infinitum, and overstaffing.
The only way Microsoft has managed to hire so many people has been by lowering their hiring standards significantly.

In the early nineties Microsoft looked at IBM, especially the bloated OS/2 team, as a case study of what not to do; somehow in the fifteen year period from 1991 – 2006 they became the bloated monster that takes five years to ship an incoherent upgrade to their flagship product.
key_insight: Organizations that grow too fast inevitably lower their hiring bar and drown in bureaucracy, becoming the very bloated monsters they once mocked.
